explanation: Under §183(e), a taxpayer can elect to postpone the hobby/business determination until after the presumption period (5 or 7 years) ends, keeping the statute of limitations open. Why option A is CORRECT - Under IRC §183(e)(4), a taxpayer can elect to postpone the determination of whether an activity is engaged in for profit until after the close of the fourth tax year following the year the activity was first engaged in (or sixth year for activities involving horses). This election keeps the statute of limitations open for all years in the postponement period, allowing the IRS to assess deficiencies if the activity is ultimately deemed a hobby. 💡 Exam Tip: Remember that the election to postpone the hobby loss determination under §183(e) is a strategic decision that requires weighing the...

question: PQR Corp purchased a computer for $5,000 in 2024. It is used 60% for business and 40% personal. Using 5-year MACRS (20% first year rate), what is the depreciation deduction?
explanation: Listed property must be used more than 50% for business to use MACRS (which it is: 60%). Depreciation = $5,000 × 20% × 60% business use = $600. Why option D is CORRECT - The correct depreciation deduction is calculated by multiplying the asset's cost by the MACRS depreciation rate and the business use percentage. In this case, $5,000 (cost) × 20% (MACRS rate) × 60% (business use) = $600. This reflects the portion of the asset's depreciation that is deductible for business purposes. 💡 Exam Tip: Remember to always consider the business use percentage when calculating depreciation for assets used for both business and personal purposes. Pay close attention to the facts provided in the question to determine the correct percentage to apply.

question: John Smith, EA, received $5,000 from a client on March 1, 2024, designated for the client's upcoming tax payment due April 15, 2024. According to Circular 230, how must John handle these funds?
options: The funds can be deposited in the practitioner's general account, provided a detailed ledger is maintained to track the client's funds separately.
The funds must be deposited in client trust accounts and disbursed promptly
No special rules apply as long as the funds are remitted to the taxing authority before the filing deadline and proper documentation is retained.
Only cash transactions exceeding $10,000 are regulated under Circular 230; other forms of payment are subject to standard accounting practices.
explanation: Client funds must be placed in identifiable accounts and disbursed promptly. Circular 230 §10.23 and ethical requirements mandate trust account handling to protect client assets. Why option B is CORRECT - Circular 230 §10.23 mandates that client funds received for tax payments must be deposited into a separate, identifiable trust account. These funds must be disbursed promptly to the IRS or the client, ensuring proper handling and accountability. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, always remember that Circular 230 prioritizes client protection and ethical conduct; any option that suggests lax handling of client funds is likely incorrect.

question:
Maria is a lawyer considering the implications of the QBI deduction for her practice. A specified service trade or business (SSTB) includes:

A) Real estate businesses
B) Manufacturing businesses
C) Health, law, accounting, consulting, financial services, and similar professional services
D) Retail establishments
options: Real estate businesses involved in property management and brokerage activities, due to their reliance on tangible assets.
Manufacturing businesses that directly produce tangible personal property and engage in substantial transformation activities.
Health, law, accounting, consulting, financial services, and similar professional services
Retail establishments selling goods directly to consumers, provided they do not offer professional services alongside the sales.
explanation: SSTBs include health, law, accounting, actuarial services, performing arts, consulting, athletics, financial services, brokerage, and any business where the principal asset is reputation or skill of employees. Why option C is CORRECT - Specified service trade or businesses (SSTBs) include businesses performing services in the fields of health, law, accounting, actuarial science, performing arts, consulting, athletics, financial services, brokerage services, or any trade or business where the principal asset of such trade or business is the reputation or skill of one or more of its employees or owners. These are considered 'service' businesses where the income is derived from the skills of the individuals involved. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the taxpayer's income...

question:
John Smith failed to file his 2018 individual income tax return. The IRS is now considering whether to pursue the unfiled return. What is the statute of limitations if a taxpayer never files a return?
A) 6 years
B) 3 years
C) 10 years
D) No statute - assessment can occur at any time
options: 6 years - the standard assessment period, extended due to non-filing
3 years - the standard assessment period from when the return was due
10 years - the period for collection after assessment of a tax liability
No statute - assessment can occur at any time
explanation: If no return is filed, or if a fraudulent return is filed, there is no statute of limitations. The IRS can assess tax at any time. Why option A is CORRECT - When a taxpayer fails to file a return, the statute of limitations on assessment remains open indefinitely. This is because the IRS has no starting point to begin its assessment process without a return. 💡 Exam Tip: Remember that the failure to file a tax return is a significant issue that eliminates the statute of limitations for assessment, so always verify filing status when representing a client with potential tax liabilities.

question: Sarah owns 60% of the stock in Beta Corp. She is considering redeeming some of her shares. For the redemption to be treated as a sale of stock rather than a dividend, it must meet certain requirements. Which of the following conditions would qualify the redemption as a sale?
options: Is substantially disproportionate or completely terminates the shareholder's interest
Is pro-rata among all shareholders, maintaining their percentage ownership and control.
Exceeds $25,000 and is reported as a capital gain on the shareholder's individual income tax return.
Occurs within 2 years of incorporation, allowing for adjustments to initial capital contributions.
explanation: A redemption receives sale treatment (capital gain) if it is substantially disproportionate, completely terminates the shareholder's interest, or is not essentially equivalent to a dividend. Attribution rules apply. Why option A is CORRECT - A stock redemption is treated as a sale (resulting in capital gain or loss) if it qualifies under specific exceptions to the general rule that redemptions are treated as dividends. Two key exceptions are a substantially disproportionate redemption under IRC Section 302(b)(2) or a complete termination of the shareholder's interest under IRC Section 302(b)(3). 💡 Exam Tip: Remember to consider the attribution rules when determining if a redemption qualifies as substantially disproportionate or a complete termination of interest; family members' stock ...

question: Maria Rodriguez is a tax preparer at Rodriguez & Associates. She needs to ensure she is compliant with all IRS regulations. PTINs must be renewed:
options: Every 5 years
Every 3 years
Once and they are permanent
Annually
explanation: PTINs must be renewed annually. The renewal period typically opens in mid-October for the following calendar year. There is a fee for initial application and renewal. Why option B is CORRECT - PTINs must be renewed annually. This requirement helps the IRS maintain an accurate and current database of all paid tax preparers, which is essential for oversight and compliance enforcement under Circular 230. 💡 Exam Tip: Remember that failing to renew your PTIN can prevent you from preparing or assisting in the preparation of federal tax returns for compensation, a critical aspect of an Enrolled Agent's practice.

question: The partners at Smith & Jones, a law firm, are evaluating the benefits of their state's pass-through entity (PTE) tax election. Some states allow pass-through entity (PTE) tax elections where:
options: The entity pays state tax and owners get federal deduction for state taxes paid
The partners avoid making federal estimated tax payments on their share of the entity's income.
The entity pays no state income tax because the tax liability is shifted entirely to the partners.
Partners pay state income tax directly, bypassing the entity, and claim a credit on their federal return.
explanation: PTE elections (workaround to $10,000 SALT cap) allow the entity to pay state tax, which the entity deducts for federal purposes. Owners reduce their distributive share by taxes paid. Why option A is CORRECT - Pass-through entity (PTE) tax elections are a workaround to the federal limitation on the state and local tax (SALT) deduction. By electing to pay state income tax at the entity level, the PTE can deduct the full amount of state taxes paid as a business expense, effectively bypassing the individual SALT deduction limit of $10,000. The owners then reduce their distributive share of income by the amount of state taxes paid on their behalf. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, remember that PTE elections are primarily a state tax strategy with federal income tax implications related to the de...

question:
Chen & Associates, a tax firm, is preparing to represent a new client, Vista Healthcare LLC. To ensure clarity and avoid future disputes, which of the following BEST describes the necessary components of engagement letters for tax representation?

A) Are prohibited
B) Should define scope of services, fees, responsibilities of both parties, and limitations
C) Are only required for court appearances
D) Cannot limit liability
options: Are prohibited due to potential conflicts of interest and perceived limitations on client rights, except in very specific circumstances.
Should define scope of services, fees, responsibilities of both parties, and limitations
Are only required for court appearances or when explicitly mandated by the IRS for specific audit representations and sensitive tax matters.
Cannot limit liability for gross negligence or intentional misconduct, but can reasonably define the scope of work to manage potential exposure.
explanation: Engagement letters protect both practitioner and client by clearly defining the scope, fees, responsibilities, and limitations. They help avoid misunderstandings and provide documentation of the agreement. Why option B is CORRECT - Engagement letters are crucial for establishing a clear understanding between the tax professional and the client. They should explicitly outline the scope of services to be provided (e.g., tax preparation, audit representation), the fees for those services, the responsibilities of both the practitioner and the client (e.g., providing necessary documentation), and any limitations on the representation. This helps prevent misunderstandings and disputes. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, always choose the answer that emphasizes clear communication, documentation, an...

question: Robert submitted an offer in compromise (OIC) to the IRS to settle his tax debt of $75,000 for a lower amount. An offer in compromise may be accepted based on:
options: The taxpayer's demonstration of significant political connections to influence the IRS decision.
The taxpayer's legally binding promise to pay the full original tax liability at a later, unspecified date.
Severe economic hardship impacting the taxpayer's ability to meet basic living expenses, without other factors.
Doubt as to collectibility, doubt as to liability, or effective tax administration
explanation: The IRS accepts OICs based on: (1) doubt as to liability - uncertainty about the correct tax, (2) doubt as to collectibility - doubt the tax can be collected in full, or (3) effective tax administration - exceptional hardship. Why option B is CORRECT - An Offer in Compromise (OIC) can be accepted by the IRS based on three grounds: doubt as to liability (uncertainty the tax is correct), doubt as to collectibility (doubt the tax can be collected in full), or effective tax administration (exceptional circumstances, such as economic hardship, warrant acceptance). These are the only legally permissible bases for accepting an OIC. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, remember that the IRS has specific criteria for accepting an OIC; you must demonstrate one of the three acceptable bases (doubt as to l...

question: An employer failed to remit $25,000 in withheld income taxes and $15,000 in employee FICA to the IRS. What is the maximum Trust Fund Recovery Penalty?
explanation: TFRP = 100% of trust fund taxes. Trust fund = withheld income tax + employee share of FICA. $25,000 + $15,000 = $40,000. Why option D is CORRECT - The Trust Fund Recovery Penalty (TFRP) is equal to 100% of the unpaid trust fund taxes. Trust fund taxes consist of withheld income taxes and the employee's share of FICA taxes. In this case, $25,000 (withheld income taxes) + $15,000 (employee FICA) = $40,000. Therefore, the maximum TFRP is $40,000. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the wording of TFRP questions. Be sure to identify all components of the trust fund taxes (withheld income tax, employee FICA, and sometimes excise taxes) and remember the penalty is 100% of that amount. Also, remember that the IRS can pursue multiple individuals within a company for the TFRP, bu...

question: Partner Y sells property to a partnership in which Y has a 60% interest for $100,000. Y's basis in the property was $120,000. What is the tax treatment?
options: Recognize $20,000 loss as a capital loss, subject to Section 1231 netting rules.
Recognize $20,000 loss as an ordinary loss because Y controls over 50% of the partnership.
No loss allowed; treated as a contribution to the partnership, increasing Y's basis.
No loss recognized; deferred until partner sells partnership interest
explanation: Under IRC §707(b)(1), losses are disallowed on sales between a partner and partnership if the partner owns more than 50%. The partnership takes a carryover basis and can recognize the loss when selling to an unrelated party. Why option D is CORRECT - IRC §707(b)(1) states that no loss shall be allowed in the case of a sale or exchange of property (other than an interest in the partnership), directly or indirectly, between a partnership and a person who owns, directly or indirectly, more than 50% of the capital interest, or profits interest, in such partnership. The loss is disallowed at the time of the sale and effectively deferred. The partnership's basis in the property is a carryover basis from the partner. 💡 Exam Tip: Remember the 50% threshold for disallowed losses in transactions...

question: The IRS has notified David Miller via mail that they need documentation to support a deduction claimed on his tax return. An IRS correspondence audit is typically used for: A) Complex business returns with multiple entities | B) Simple issues that can be resolved through mail, such as documentation of deductions | C) International tax matters | D) Criminal investigations
options: Complex business returns with multiple entities, requiring detailed examination of financial statements and intercompany transactions.
Simple issues that can be resolved through mail, such as documentation of deductions
International tax matters involving cross-border transactions, transfer pricing, and foreign tax credits that necessitate specialized expertise.
Criminal investigations involving suspected tax evasion or fraud, which are handled by IRS Criminal Investigation division agents.
explanation: Correspondence audits are the simplest type of IRS examination, conducted entirely by mail. They typically address one or two issues that can be resolved with documentation, such as verification of charitable contributions, itemized deductions, or withholding credits. Office audits require the taxpayer to visit an IRS office, while field audits are conducted at the taxpayer's home or place of business. Why option B is CORRECT - Correspondence audits are designed for simple issues that can be resolved through the mail, such as substantiating deductions or credits. The IRS uses this method for efficiency when only a few documents are needed to verify information on a tax return. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, remember that the IRS aims for efficiency; correspondence audits are their go-to f...

question: Global Investments Inc. sold a depreciated building for a gain. What is the maximum tax rate applied to any unrecaptured Section 1250 gain?
explanation: Unrecaptured §1250 gain (straight-line depreciation on real property) is taxed at a maximum rate of 25%, while the remaining gain is taxed at regular LTCG rates (0%, 15%, or 20%). Why option D is CORRECT - Unrecaptured Section 1250 gain, which is generally the amount of straight-line depreciation taken on real property, is taxed at a maximum rate of 25%. Any remaining gain beyond the unrecaptured Section 1250 gain is taxed at the applicable long-term capital gains rate (0%, 15%, or 20%). 💡 Exam Tip: Remember that unrecaptured Section 1250 gain only applies to real property and is limited to the amount of depreciation taken. On the exam, carefully identify if the gain is related to real property and if depreciation was claimed to determine if the 25% rate applies.

question: In a liquidating distribution, Partner H (basis $80,000) receives equipment (inside basis $30,000) and land (inside basis $40,000). How is H's basis allocated?
options: $30,000 to equipment, $40,000 to land, $10,000 unallocated
$40,000 to equipment, $40,000 to land
$30,000 to equipment, $50,000 to land
$34,286 to equipment, $45,714 to land (proportional)
explanation: Under IRC §732(c), when multiple properties are received and outside basis exceeds inside bases, the excess ($10,000) is allocated proportionally based on FMV. Here, each takes its inside basis plus proportional share of excess. Why option D is CORRECT - When a partner receives property in a liquidating distribution and their outside basis exceeds the inside basis of the distributed assets, the excess basis is allocated proportionally based on the relative inside bases of the assets. Here, the equipment receives $30,000 + ($10,000 * (30,000/70,000)) = $34,286, and the land receives $40,000 + ($10,000 * (40,000/70,000)) = $45,714. 💡 Exam Tip: Remember the basis allocation rules for liquidating distributions: first, allocate basis up to the asset's inside basis; then, allocate any remain...

question: GreenTech Solutions failed to remit payroll taxes withheld from its employees' wages. The IRS is considering assessing the Trust Fund Recovery Penalty (TFRP). The TFRP may be assessed against:
options: Any responsible person who willfully failed to pay over trust fund taxes
Only the individual who physically signed the payroll tax returns submitted to the IRS.
Solely the employer entity, as the legal entity responsible for all business tax obligations.
Exclusively the owner of the business, as the individual with ultimate control over its assets.
explanation: The TFRP (100% penalty) can be assessed against any "responsible person" who willfully failed to collect, account for, or pay over trust fund taxes (employee withholding). This includes officers, directors, employees, or others with authority over finances. Multiple persons can be liable. Why option A is CORRECT - The Trust Fund Recovery Penalty (TFRP) can be assessed against any 'responsible person' who willfully failed to collect, account for, or pay over trust fund taxes, as defined under IRC Section 6672. A responsible person has the duty to collect, account for, and pay over these taxes, and their failure to do so willfully makes them personally liable. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, remember that 'willfulness' in the context of the TFRP doesn't necessarily require malicious intent; ...

question: Fine Art Gallery displays paintings owned by various artists. The gallery only earns a commission when a painting is sold. In whose inventory are these paintings included?
options: The consignee (party holding the goods)
Neither party until sold
The consignor (owner of the goods)
Both parties equally
explanation: Consigned goods remain in the inventory of the consignor (owner) until sold. The consignee holds the goods for sale on behalf of the consignor but does not own them. Why option C is CORRECT - The consignor retains ownership of the goods held on consignment until they are sold to a third party. Consequently, the goods remain part of the consignor's inventory and are reported on their tax return until the sale takes place. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the terms 'consignor' and 'consignee' to correctly identify who owns the inventory for tax reporting purposes. Misidentifying these roles is a common error.

question: Which of the following is NOT a valid reason for requesting penalty abatement based on reasonable cause?
options: Death or serious illness of the taxpayer or a member of their immediate family
Unavoidable absence of the taxpayer
Lack of funds to pay the tax due
Destruction of records due to fire or other casualty
explanation: Lack of funds, by itself, is generally not considered reasonable cause for penalty abatement. The other options are valid reasons if they prevented the taxpayer from fulfilling their tax obligations. 💡 Exam Tip: When answering penalty abatement questions, focus on whether the situation presented was truly outside the taxpayer's control and prevented them from meeting their tax obligations. Look for keywords like 'unavoidable,' 'unexpected,' or 'reasonable care'.

question: GreenTech Solutions provides consulting services. They completed a project for $50,000 in December, invoiced the client, but haven't received payment. Under the accrual method, when is the $50,000 income generally recognized?
options: When an invoice is issued to the client, assuming proper documentation and internal controls are in place to support the revenue recognition.
When cash is received from the client, reflecting the actual inflow of funds and providing a clear audit trail for verification purposes.
All events have occurred to fix the right to receive and the amount can be determined with reasonable accuracy
When services are substantially complete, signifying that the core obligations have been fulfilled, even if minor tasks or adjustments remain outstanding.
explanation: Under the accrual method, income is recognized when all events have occurred that fix the right to receive it (all-events test) and the amount can be determined with reasonable accuracy. Why option C is CORRECT - The accrual method of accounting, as defined by the all-events test, recognizes income when (1) all events have occurred that fix the right to receive the income, and (2) the amount of income can be determined with reasonable accuracy. This ensures income is matched to the period in which it is earned, regardless of when cash is received. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, carefully consider whether a question is asking about the cash or accrual method, as the timing of income and expense recognition differs significantly between the two.

question: David formed a single-member LLC, called 'David's Detailing'. By default, how is David's Detailing taxed for federal income tax purposes?
options: A C Corporation, requiring filing Form 1120 and subject to corporate income tax rates.
A Partnership, requiring filing Form 1065 and allocating income/losses to David as a partner.
An S Corporation, requiring filing Form 1120-S and potentially offering pass-through taxation.
Disregarded entity (sole proprietorship)
explanation: A single-member LLC is a disregarded entity by default and is taxed as a sole proprietorship. The owner reports income and expenses on Schedule C. The LLC can elect to be taxed as a corporation if desired. Why option B is CORRECT - By default, the IRS treats a single-member LLC as an entity disregarded as separate from its owner under Treasury Regulation §301.7701-3. This means the LLC's activities are reported directly on the owner's individual income tax return, typically on Schedule C (Profit or Loss from Business) as if it were a sole proprietorship. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the number of members in an LLC, as this is a key determinant of its default tax classification. Remember that elections to change tax status must be made timely and using the correct ...

question: Jane Doe, an Enrolled Agent, prepared 250 tax returns this year. How long should she retain records of the returns she prepared, considering potential IRS inquiries or other regulatory requirements?
options: Indefinitely, to ensure full compliance with all potential future tax law changes and audits.
For at least 3 years (or longer as provided by other rules)
Only 1 year, as that is the standard statute of limitations for amended returns.
No retention is required unless specifically requested by the client in writing.
explanation: IRC §6107(b) requires preparers to retain completed copy or list (names, TINs, tax years) for 3 years after the return due date or filing date. Longer retention may be prudent for malpractice defense. Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6107(b) mandates that tax return preparers retain either a completed copy of the return or a record (list) containing the name, taxpayer identification number (TIN), and taxable year of the return for at least three years from the return due date or the date the return was presented to the taxpayer for signature, whichever is later. Furthermore, other rules, such as state requirements or statutes of limitations for legal claims, may necessitate longer retention periods. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific timeframes mentioned in t...

question: The IRS suspects potential tax fraud by John Smith, and his case is referred to Criminal Investigation (CI). What happens to the civil assessment statute of limitations during the CI investigation?
options: Civil collection continues normally, but only for amounts not related to the suspected fraud.
The assessment statute is suspended during the investigation
The taxpayer is immediately imprisoned pending the outcome of the criminal investigation and subsequent trial.
All penalties are waived to encourage cooperation with the Criminal Investigation division, regardless of findings.
explanation: When a case is referred to Criminal Investigation, the assessment statute is suspended during any period a JAG letter (criminal investigation letter) is outstanding. Civil collection generally pauses during active criminal investigation. Why option B is CORRECT - The assessment statute is suspended during the period a criminal investigation is ongoing, specifically when a JAG (Judge Advocate General) letter, indicating a criminal investigation, is outstanding. This suspension prevents the IRS from being time-barred from assessing taxes if the criminal investigation uncovers additional tax liabilities. IRC Section 6503(h) addresses suspension of limitations periods when a pending criminal case impacts civil tax matters. 💡 Exam Tip: Remember that the issuance of a JAG letter is the trigg...

question:
Mr. Miller received a correspondence audit notice from the IRS requesting documentation to support deductions claimed on Schedule C. What should Mr. Miller do?

A) Ignore the notice and wait for a phone call
B) Respond by the date shown on the notice with the requested documents and any supporting information
C) Call the IRS to verbally explain the items
D) File an amended return immediately
options: Ignore the notice, assuming the IRS will follow up with a phone call if further clarification is needed, thus saving time and potential confusion.
Respond by the date shown on the notice with the requested documents and any supporting information
Call the IRS and attempt to verbally explain the items in question, hoping to resolve the audit quickly and avoid the hassle of gathering documentation.
File an amended return immediately, preemptively correcting any potential errors and demonstrating good faith to the IRS, potentially avoiding further scrutiny.
explanation: When a taxpayer receives a correspondence audit notice, they should respond by the deadline stated in the notice (typically 30 days) and include copies (not originals) of the requested documentation. The response should be mailed to the address on the notice, or faxed if a fax number is provided. If the taxpayer needs more time, they should contact the IRS before the deadline to request an extension. Failing to respond will result in the IRS making adjustments based on available information. Why option B is CORRECT - Responding by the date shown on the notice with the requested documents and any supporting information is the correct procedure for a correspondence audit. This allows the taxpayer to present their case and potentially avoid an unfavorable adjustment. Circular 230 requires ...

question: Accountant Michael Davis is completing Form 2848 for his client, Lisa Miller. He needs to indicate his professional designation on the form. The representative designation code "e" on Form 2848 identifies:
options: An Enrolled Agent
An attorney
A CPA
An Enrolled Actuary
explanation: Form 2848 uses letter codes to identify representative designations: a = Attorney, b = CPA, c = Enrolled Agent, d = Officer (for corporate matters), e = Full-Time Employee, etc. Why option A is CORRECT - Form 2848, Power of Attorney and Declaration of Representative, uses specific designation codes to identify the type of representative authorized to act on behalf of a taxpayer. The designation code 'e' is specifically assigned to Enrolled Agents, who are federally-authorized tax practitioners. 💡 Exam Tip: Memorize the primary representative designation codes on Form 2848 (a, b, c, d, e) as questions often test your familiarity with this form and its proper usage in representation scenarios.

question: Precision Manufacturing offers a defined benefit plan to its employees. The plan promises a specific monthly payment upon retirement. In a defined benefit plan:
options: The benefit is determined by a formula (typically based on salary and service)
The employee's retirement benefit is determined by the accumulated account balance, reflecting investment gains and losses.
Only employee contributions are allowed, with the employee directing investments among a range of mutual fund options.
Contributions are limited to $23,000 per year, adjusted annually for inflation, mirroring limits in defined contribution plans.
explanation: Defined benefit plans promise a specific retirement benefit calculated by a formula, typically based on compensation and years of service. The employer funds the plan to meet the promised benefit. Why option A is CORRECT - Defined benefit plans are structured to provide a pre-determined retirement income, calculated using a formula that typically considers factors such as the employee's salary history and years of service. The employer bears the investment risk and is responsible for ensuring sufficient funds are available to meet the promised benefits. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the wording of retirement plan questions. Distinguish between 'defined benefit' and 'defined contribution' plans, and remember who bears the investment risk in each type.

question: A tax preparer must retain a copy of a tax return or claim for refund for how many years?
options: 2 years
3 years
5 years
1 year
explanation: A tax preparer must retain a copy of a tax return or claim for refund (or a list of the returns prepared) for a period of 3 years from the return due date or filing date, whichever is later. 💡 Exam Tip: Pay close attention to the specific wording of the question. It's asking about the preparer's retention requirement, not the taxpayer's. Also, be aware that there are different retention periods for different types of records, so focus on the context of the question.

question: A long-time client of Chen & Associates requests assistance with a complex international tax issue, an area where the EA lacks expertise. If the EA is asked to handle a matter outside their competence: A) They should always accept to help the client | B) They may accept if they become competent through study or associate with someone competent | C) They must always decline | D) Competence is not considered under Circular 230
options: They should always accept to help the client, as client retention is paramount and expertise can be developed on the job.
They may accept if they become competent through study or associate with someone competent
They must always decline, referring the client to another professional, as any attempt to learn would violate Circular 230.
Competence is not explicitly considered under Circular 230; the focus is solely on due diligence and avoiding conflicts of interest.
explanation: Under Circular 230 §10.35, practitioners must be competent to handle matters. If not initially competent, they may become so through reasonable preparation or associate with someone who is competent. Why option B is CORRECT - Circular 230 §10.35(a) allows an EA to accept a matter outside their initial competence if they can achieve competence through reasonable preparation (study) or by associating with a competent practitioner. This protects clients while allowing EAs to expand their expertise. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to scenarios involving competence. Look for keywords like 'reasonable preparation,' 'associate,' or 'due diligence' to identify the correct course of action under Circular 230.

question:
After receiving an adverse determination from the IRS regarding a deduction claimed on their 2022 tax return, the partners of Willow Creek Partnership are considering their options. Which of the following accurately describes the rights available to them to appeal the IRS's decision?

A) Only state court remedies
B) Administrative appeals through IRS Appeals and judicial review in various courts depending on the issue
C) Only Tax Court options
D) No formal process
options: Exclusive jurisdiction lies solely with state courts, as federal tax matters are determined by state partnership law and contract interpretation.
Administrative appeals through IRS Appeals and judicial review in various courts depending on the issue
The partners are limited to petitioning the United States Tax Court, which has exclusive jurisdiction over all partnership-related tax disputes.
There is no formal appeal process available, as the IRS's initial determination is final and binding on all partners of the Willow Creek Partnership.
explanation: Taxpayers have rights to: (1) administrative appeal to IRS Independent Office of Appeals (audit, collection, penalty), and (2) judicial review (Tax Court for deficiency, district/claims court for refund, etc.). Why option B is CORRECT - Taxpayers have the right to both administrative appeals through the IRS Independent Office of Appeals and judicial review in courts such as the Tax Court, U.S. District Courts, and the U.S. Court of Federal Claims. The specific court depends on the type of tax issue and whether the taxpayer is seeking a redetermination of a deficiency or a refund. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, remember that the choice of court for judicial review often hinges on whether the taxpayer wants to litigate *before* paying the tax (Tax Court) or is seeking a refund of taxes alre...

question: Lisa received a letter from the IRS providing incorrect guidance on a complex tax issue. She followed the advice, resulting in a penalty. Penalty relief for erroneous written advice from the IRS requires the taxpayer:
options: Only to claim they received bad advice from an IRS employee, regardless of how the advice was solicited or documented.
Read an IRS publication incorrectly and applied that misunderstanding to their tax situation without seeking further clarification.
Spoke to any IRS employee at a public event and acted upon their off-the-cuff remarks without any further verification.
Reasonably relied on written advice from the IRS in response to a specific written request
explanation: Under IRC §6404(f), penalties may be abated if the taxpayer reasonably relied on erroneous written advice from the IRS in response to a specific written request containing accurate and complete information. Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6404(f) specifically allows for penalty abatement when a taxpayer reasonably relied on erroneous written advice from the IRS. This advice must be in direct response to a specific written request from the taxpayer containing accurate and complete information. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific requirements for penalty abatement, especially the need for *written* advice from the IRS in response to a *written* request containing complete and accurate information.

question: Maria Rodriguez needs to obtain a transcript of her 2023 tax return to verify her income for a loan application. Which IRS form should she use? Form 4506-T is used to:
options: Request a transcript of tax return or account information
Authorize a power of attorney to represent you before the IRS
Request a copy of a tax return, including all attachments and schedules
File an amended return to correct errors or make changes to a previously filed return
explanation: Form 4506-T (Request for Transcript of Tax Return) is the free form used to request various types of tax transcripts from the IRS. Why option A is CORRECT - Form 4506-T, Request for Transcript of Tax Return, is specifically designed to allow taxpayers (or their authorized representatives) to obtain various types of transcripts from the IRS, including tax return transcripts, tax account transcripts, wage and income documents, and verification of non-filing. These transcripts provide a summary of tax information rather than a complete copy of the return. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific wording of questions about IRS forms. Distinguish between 'transcript' and 'copy' as they require different forms (4506-T vs. 4506).

question: Mark wants to start a business and minimize his self-employment tax. Which business structure, if properly implemented, allows the owner to minimize self-employment tax on business income?
options: Sole proprietorship (with expense deductions)
Single-member LLC (treated as a disregarded entity)
General partnership (allocating profits as desired)
S corporation (with reasonable salary)
explanation: S corporation shareholders who work in the business must receive reasonable salary (subject to FICA), but distributions above that are not subject to SE tax. Sole proprietors and partners pay SE tax on all net earnings. Why option D is CORRECT - An S corporation allows owners who are also employees to split their compensation between a reasonable salary (subject to FICA taxes) and distributions. Distributions are not subject to self-employment tax, potentially minimizing the overall self-employment tax burden. The key is that the salary must be deemed 'reasonable' by the IRS, considering the services provided. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, remember that the IRS closely examines S corporation shareholder salaries. Be prepared to justify the 'reasonableness' of the salary based on industry...

question: Sterling Enterprises liquidated its subsidiary, Diamond Corp., under Section 332. How does Diamond Corp. treat the distribution of assets to Sterling Enterprises in the liquidation?
options: Recognizes no gain or loss on distribution to the 80% parent
Files a final consolidated return showing capital gain, calculated as if an arm's length sale occurred.
Recognizes all gains and losses on distributed assets as if sold at fair market value to an unrelated third party.
Recognizes gain but not loss, deferring the gain until Sterling Enterprises disposes of the acquired assets.
explanation: Under IRC §337(a), a subsidiary recognizes no gain or loss on distributions to an 80% distributee in a Section 332 liquidation. However, gain may be recognized on distributions to minority shareholders. Why option A is CORRECT - In a Section 332 liquidation, IRC §337(a) explicitly states that the subsidiary corporation recognizes no gain or loss on the distribution of property to its 80% or more corporate parent. This non-recognition rule is a key component of tax-free corporate reorganizations and liquidations. 💡 Exam Tip: Remember that the 80% ownership threshold is critical for a Section 332 liquidation to qualify for tax-free treatment at the subsidiary level; if the parent owns less than 80%, the liquidation will be taxable.

question: Two partners in the RST Partnership sold their entire interests to a new investor. The total interest sold was 60%. Under current law (TCJA), what is the impact of this sale on the RST Partnership?
options: Requires revaluation of all partnership assets under section 743(b) if a valid election is in place
Occurs when 50% or more interests are sold within 12 months
No longer exists - TCJA repealed technical termination rules
Triggers recognition of built-in gains only if the partnership has a section 754 election in effect
explanation: TCJA repealed the technical termination rules for partnerships effective for tax years beginning after 2017. Previously, a 50%+ sale of interests within 12 months caused a termination. Why option C is CORRECT - The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) repealed the technical termination rules for partnerships under Section 708(b)(1)(B), effective for tax years beginning after December 31, 2017. This means that a sale or exchange of 50% or more of the total interest in partnership capital and profits within a 12-month period no longer automatically terminates the partnership for tax purposes. 💡 Exam Tip: Remember that the TCJA significantly altered partnership taxation. Be prepared to identify issues that were relevant under pre-TCJA law but are no longer applicable, especially regarding partner...

question: A taxpayer has the right to request a Collection Due Process (CDP) hearing when the IRS:
options: Issues a refund.
Sends a notice of deficiency.
Levies their wages or bank account.
Extends the filing deadline.
explanation: A taxpayer has the right to a CDP hearing when the IRS levies their wages or bank account to collect unpaid taxes. 💡 Exam Tip: Remember that CDP rights are triggered by specific IRS collection actions, primarily levies and liens. If a question mentions a levy or lien, immediately consider CDP as a potential answer. Also, CDP hearings are *before* the collection action becomes permanent.

question: Apex Distribution sold a delivery truck this year for more than its adjusted basis. What is the general rule for Section 1245 recapture?
options: Capped at $100,000 per year, with any excess depreciation carried forward and subject to a 20% tax rate.
Full recapture - 100% of depreciation taken (up to gain)
Limited to additional depreciation over straight-line
Only 60% of depreciation is subject to recapture as ordinary income, with the remaining 40% taxed at capital gains rates.
explanation: Section 1245 requires full recapture - all depreciation is recaptured as ordinary income, up to the amount of gain recognized. Unlike §1250, there is no limitation to additional depreciation. Why option B is CORRECT - Section 1245 mandates full recapture of depreciation. This means that when Section 1245 property (generally personal property) is sold at a gain, the gain is treated as ordinary income to the extent of depreciation previously taken. The remaining gain, if any, may be treated as Section 1231 gain. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, carefully distinguish between Section 1245 (personal property) and Section 1250 (real property) recapture rules, as the treatment of depreciation recapture differs significantly between the two.

question:
GreenTech Solutions filed its 2022 corporate tax return on March 15, 2023, but did not pay the $50,000 balance due until June 15, 2023. How is interest calculated on this underpayment?
A) Never accrues
B) Runs from the due date of the return until paid, compounded daily
C) Is a flat 10% per year
D) Stops after 3 years
options: Never accrues if the underpayment is less than $100 and paid within 30 days.
Runs from the due date of the return until paid, compounded daily
Is a flat 10% per year, calculated only on the original tax due amount.
Stops after 3 years from the original due date of the return, regardless of payment.
explanation: Interest on underpayments runs from the original due date (without extensions) until the date of payment. The rate is the federal short-term rate plus 3% (5% for large corporate underpayments), compounded daily. Why option B is CORRECT - Interest on underpayments runs from the original due date of the return (without regard to extensions) until the date of payment. The interest is compounded daily, reflecting the time value of money and the IRS's need to have funds available. 💡 Exam Tip: Remember to distinguish between the statute of limitations for assessment (which limits how long the IRS has to assess a tax) and the accrual of interest on underpayments (which continues until the tax is paid).

question: Which of the following is *not* a method the IRS commonly uses to collect unpaid taxes?
options: Levy
Garnishment
Seizure
Lottery
explanation: The IRS uses levies, garnishments, and seizures to collect unpaid taxes. The IRS does not use a lottery to collect taxes. 💡 Exam Tip: When identifying incorrect collection methods, focus on options that seem unusual or outside the realm of standard government procedure. The IRS typically uses direct, legally established methods, not unconventional or random approaches.

question: Maria Sanchez, EA, is representing a client, Robert Miller, who has become increasingly uncooperative, refusing to provide necessary documentation and frequently missing appointments. Mr. Miller also hasn't paid his agreed-upon fees for the last two months. Under Circular 230, Maria MAY withdraw from representation:
options: Only with IRS permission after submitting Form 2848 and a detailed explanation of the reasons for withdrawal, subject to IRS review.
When client renders representation unreasonably difficult, fails to pay agreed fees, or for good cause
Never without client consent, unless a court order is obtained demonstrating that continued representation would be unethical or illegal for the practitioner.
Only at year end, coinciding with the finalization of annual tax returns and after providing the client with a 30-day notice of intent to withdraw.
explanation: Permissive withdrawal is allowed when the client substantially fails to fulfill obligations, makes representation unreasonably difficult, or when other good cause exists for terminating the relationship. Why option B is CORRECT - Circular 230, Section 10.36(b), explicitly allows a practitioner to withdraw from representation if the client acts in a manner that renders the representation unreasonably difficult, fails to substantially fulfill an obligation to the practitioner (such as paying agreed-upon fees), or if other good cause for withdrawal exists. This provides flexibility for practitioners facing challenging client situations. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the wording of questions related to Circular 230. Distinguish between mandatory and permissive actions,...

question: During an audit of Miller Manufacturing, disagreements arose regarding certain expense deductions. Miller's representative wants to resolve the issues quickly. What is Fast Track Settlement (FTS)?
options: A program allowing Appeals participation during the examination
A streamlined process only available for C corporations with assets exceeding $10 million.
A specialized court proceeding designed for expedited resolution of complex tax disputes involving transfer pricing.
A mandatory IRS procedure required before filing a Tax Court petition for cases exceeding $500,000.
explanation: Fast Track Settlement brings an Appeals officer into the examination while it's still with the exam function. Available for most issues, it aims to resolve disputes quickly without the traditional appeals process. Why option A is CORRECT - Fast Track Settlement (FTS) is indeed a program that allows taxpayers to involve Appeals during the examination phase. This early involvement aims to facilitate quicker resolution of disputes by bringing in an independent perspective while the case is still being developed. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, remember that Fast Track Settlement is a voluntary process, and knowing the eligibility requirements and the types of issues suitable for FTS can help you advise clients effectively.

question: If the IRS places an account in Currently Not Collectible status, what happens to the statute of limitations?
options: It is extended by 6 years
It is suspended
It is reset to 10 years
It continues to run
explanation: CNC status does NOT suspend the statute. The 10-year collection period continues to run while the account is in CNC status. Why option D is CORRECT - The 10-year collection statute of limitations continues to run even when an account is placed in Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status. CNC status simply means the IRS has determined that, at the present time, the taxpayer cannot afford to pay the tax liability. The IRS can still pursue collection efforts until the statute expires. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific wording of questions related to the collection statute. Understand the difference between actions that suspend the statute (like bankruptcy) versus situations where it continues to run (like CNC status).

question: A taxpayer filed their return 3 months late with $10,000 tax due (no reasonable cause). What is the failure-to-file penalty?
explanation: Failure-to-file = 5% per month (or part), max 25%. 3 months × 5% = 15%. Penalty = $10,000 × 15% = $1,500. Why option A is CORRECT - The failure-to-file penalty is 5% of the unpaid taxes for each month or part of a month that the return is late, up to a maximum of 25%. Since the return was 3 months late, the penalty is 15% (3 months * 5%) of the $10,000 tax due, which equals $1,500. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the wording of penalty questions, especially the number of months late and whether reasonable cause exists, as these factors significantly impact the penalty amount.

question: Which of the following is a requirement for a tax preparer to avoid penalties related to tax positions?
options: Guarantee the accuracy of the return
Take a position only if there is substantial authority
Disclose all uncertain positions regardless of authority
Prepare the return for free
explanation: Tax preparers must have a reasonable belief that there is substantial authority for a tax position to avoid penalties. They are not required to guarantee accuracy or prepare the return for free. 💡 Exam Tip: When answering questions about preparer penalties, focus on the preparer's actions and beliefs at the time of preparing the return. Consider whether the preparer acted reasonably and in good faith, and whether they had a reasonable basis for the tax position taken. Eliminate answer choices that suggest guarantees of accuracy or free services.

question: Apex Distribution Corp. anticipates a tax liability of $750 for the upcoming year. Under IRS regulations, is Apex Distribution Corp. required to make estimated tax payments?
options: $10,000 or more
$5,000 or more
$500 or more
$1,000 or more
explanation: C corporations must make estimated tax payments if the expected tax for the year is $500 or more. Payments are due the 15th of the 4th, 6th, 9th, and 12th months. Each payment is generally 25% of required annual payment. Why option C is CORRECT - C corporations are required to make estimated tax payments if their expected tax liability for the year is $500 or more. This requirement ensures that the IRS receives tax revenue throughout the year, rather than waiting until the corporation's tax return is filed. 💡 Exam Tip: Remember the $500 threshold for C corporation estimated taxes. On the exam, carefully read the facts to determine if the corporation's estimated tax liability exceeds this amount, triggering the estimated tax payment requirement and potential penalties for underpayment.

question: Omega Corp. filed its Form 1120 three months late without requesting an extension. Omega's unpaid tax liability is $10,000. What penalty does Omega face?
options: No penalty assessed if the tax is paid before IRS initiates formal collection activity.
10% of gross receipts, capped at the amount of the unpaid tax liability.
5% of unpaid tax per month, up to 25%
A flat $500 penalty, plus interest on the underpayment from the original due date.
explanation: The failure-to-file penalty for corporations is 5% of unpaid tax per month (or fraction), up to 25% maximum. There is a minimum penalty for returns over 60 days late. Why option C is CORRECT - The failure-to-file penalty for a C corporation is indeed 5% of the unpaid taxes for each month or part of a month that the return is late, up to a maximum penalty of 25% of the unpaid taxes. This aligns with IRC Section 6651(a)(1). 💡 Exam Tip: Remember to differentiate between failure-to-file and failure-to-pay penalties, as they are calculated differently; failure-to-file is generally more severe, so prioritize timely filing for your corporate clients.

question: GreenTech Solutions' tax account has been placed in Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status due to significant financial hardship. While an account is in CNC status:
options: The CSED is suspended
A new CSED is established
The CSED continues to run
The CSED is extended by 2 years
explanation: CNC status does not suspend the collection statute. The 10-year CSED continues to run, and if it expires while the account is in CNC, the liability is written off. Why option C is CORRECT - The Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED) continues to run even when an account is placed in Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status. This means the IRS's legal authority to collect the tax liability expires on the original CSED, regardless of the CNC status. 💡 Exam Tip: Remember that CNC status is a temporary administrative designation based on a taxpayer's inability to pay; it does not alter the underlying legal timeframe for collection. Pay close attention to questions involving the CSED and how it interacts with other IRS procedures.

question: Maria Rodriguez needs to file a power of attorney for her client, John Smith. The IRS uses a unique identifier to track these authorizations. A Centralized Authorization File (CAF) number:
options: Is assigned by IRS to track practitioner authorizations and powers of attorney on file
Is the same as a PTIN, and must be renewed alongside your Preparer Tax Identification Number each year to remain valid.
Is only for attorneys who are submitting Form 2848 on behalf of their clients, as CPAs and enrolled agents use a different system.
Expires annually, requiring a new power of attorney and CAF number request to be submitted to the IRS at the beginning of each tax year.
explanation: The CAF number is assigned by IRS when a POA (Form 2848) or Tax Information Authorization (Form 8821) is first filed. It tracks all authorizations on file for the practitioner and expedites processing. Why option A is CORRECT - A Centralized Authorization File (CAF) number is a unique identifier assigned by the IRS to track a practitioner's authority to represent taxpayers. It allows the IRS to quickly locate and verify authorizations, streamlining communication and ensuring proper representation. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, remember that the CAF number is a practitioner-specific identifier, while Form 2848 (Power of Attorney) and Form 8821 (Tax Information Authorization) are client-specific authorizations.

question:
When preparing a client's tax return, a practitioner exercising due diligence under Circular 230 must:
A) Rely on client information unless the practitioner has reason to question it | B) Personally verify every item reported on a return | C) Obtain all source documents before preparing a return | D) Independently research all legal issues
options: Rely on client information unless the practitioner has reason to question it
Personally verify every item reported on a return by securing independent corroboration, regardless of the client's credibility.
Obtain all source documents before preparing a return, including bank statements, receipts, and brokerage statements, even for immaterial items.
Independently research all legal issues, including well-established precedents and IRS guidance, to ensure absolute certainty in the return's accuracy.
explanation: Circular 230 §10.34 allows practitioners to rely in good faith on client information without verification unless there is reason to believe it is incorrect or incomplete. Practitioners must make reasonable inquiries. Why option A is CORRECT - Circular 230 §10.34(d) states that a practitioner may rely in good faith without verification upon information furnished by the client. However, the practitioner cannot ignore the implications of information furnished to, or actually known by, the practitioner, and must make reasonable inquiries if the information appears to be incorrect, inconsistent, or incomplete. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, remember that 'reasonable inquiry' is the key phrase for due diligence. If a fact seems off, you must investigate further, but you aren't required to audit...

question: When a partner contributes property to a partnership in exchange for a partnership interest, the partner generally recognizes:
options: No gain or loss
Gain equal to FMV minus adjusted basis
Gain only if the FMV exceeds twice the basis
Loss only if the basis exceeds FMV
explanation: Under §721, no gain or loss is generally recognized on contribution of property to a partnership in exchange for a partnership interest. The partnership takes the contributor's basis (carryover basis) and the partner's basis in the partnership interest equals the property basis. Why option A is CORRECT - Generally, under IRC §721(a), no gain or loss is recognized by a partner or the partnership when property is contributed to the partnership in exchange for a partnership interest. This rule facilitates the formation of partnerships without triggering immediate tax consequences. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, carefully consider whether the question is asking about the *general rule* for partnership formation or a specific exception (e.g., disguised sale, contribution of services). Look for...

question: A C corporation distributes property with FMV of $80,000 and basis of $30,000 to a shareholder. The corporation:
options: Recognizes no gain on the distribution because it's a non-taxable event for the corporation.
Recognizes $30,000 gain, the amount of the distribution considered a dividend to the shareholder.
Recognizes $50,000 gain as if the property were sold at FMV
Deducts $50,000 as a dividend expense, reducing taxable income, since it's a distribution to owners.
explanation: Under IRC §311(b), when a corporation distributes appreciated property, it recognizes gain as if the property were sold for its FMV. The $50,000 gain ($80,000 - $30,000) is recognized. Why option C is CORRECT - IRC §311(b) dictates that a corporation distributing appreciated property recognizes gain as if it sold the property at its fair market value (FMV). In this case, the FMV is $80,000 and the basis is $30,000, resulting in a recognized gain of $50,000. 💡 Exam Tip: Remember that C corporations always recognize gain (but not loss) on distributions of appreciated property to shareholders. Pay close attention to the type of entity in the question stem, as the rules vary significantly between C corporations, S corporations, and partnerships.

question: A taxpayer who disagrees with the results of an IRS examination may request an appeal with the IRS Office of Appeals by filing:
options: Form 2848, Power of Attorney and Declaration of Representative, to authorize representation during the appeals process.
A written protest (for proposed adjustments over $25,000) or a small case request (for $25,000 or less)
A petition with the Tax Court within 90 days of the statutory notice of deficiency, bypassing the IRS Office of Appeals.
Form 843, Claim for Refund and Request for Abatement, to request a refund of overpaid taxes or abatement of assessed taxes.
explanation: If a taxpayer disagrees with the examination results, they may request an appeal. For proposed adjustments exceeding $25,000, a formal written protest is required. For proposed adjustments of $25,000 or less, the taxpayer may file a small case request (a brief written statement). The Appeals Office is an independent function within the IRS that resolves disputes without litigation. Why option B is CORRECT - A taxpayer initiates an appeal with the IRS Office of Appeals by filing either a written protest (for proposed adjustments exceeding $25,000) or a small case request (for proposed adjustments of $25,000 or less). These documents formally notify the IRS of the taxpayer's disagreement and desire to pursue an appeal. 💡 Exam Tip: Remember the $25,000 threshold for written protests versu...

question: After reviewing Sarah's financial situation, the IRS placed her account in CNC status. When an account is placed in CNC status:
options: All liens are converted to judgments to expedite collection efforts.
Any federal tax lien is released to provide temporary financial relief.
The federal tax lien typically remains in place
A new lien is automatically filed to ensure the IRS maintains its priority.
explanation: Federal tax liens generally remain in place when an account goes into CNC status. The lien protects the government's interest while collection is suspended. Why option C is CORRECT - When an account is placed in Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status, the IRS has determined that the taxpayer cannot afford to pay the tax debt at that time. However, the federal tax lien typically remains in place to protect the government's interest in the taxpayer's property should their financial situation improve. 💡 Exam Tip: Remember that CNC status is a temporary reprieve, not forgiveness. The IRS can resume collection activity if the taxpayer's financial situation improves, and the lien remains in effect until the debt is satisfied or the statute of limitations expires, so be prepared to advise cli...

question: Elena prepares tax returns for Ramirez & Sons. On a complex depreciation issue, she took a position the IRS deemed unreasonable. Elena derived $2,500 in income from that return. The penalty for a preparer taking an unreasonable position on a return is:
options: $100 per return, unless the client fully discloses the position.
$10,000 per return if the preparer acted with willful intent.
The greater of $1,000 or 50% of income derived from the return
$5,000 per return, reduced proportionally if the client cooperates.
explanation: If an understatement of tax is due to an unreasonable position, the preparer penalty is the greater of $1,000 or 50% of the income derived by the preparer with respect to the return. Why option C is CORRECT - The penalty for a preparer taking an unreasonable position on a return is the greater of $1,000 or 50% of the income derived (or to be derived) by the preparer with respect to the return or claim for refund. This penalty aims to deter preparers from taking aggressive positions that lack substantial authority. 💡 Exam Tip: Remember the 'greater of' rule for the unreasonable position penalty; it's a common trick on the EA exam to offer only one part of the calculation as an answer choice. Pay close attention to the wording of the question to determine if the position was disclosed or...

question: After receiving a notice of intent to levy from the IRS, Sarah requests a Collection Due Process (CDP) hearing. During the CDP hearing, Sarah can take certain actions to resolve the tax matter. Which of the following actions is Sarah permitted to take at the CDP hearing?
options: Appeal directly to the Supreme Court based on constitutional grounds, bypassing lower courts due to the severity of the tax levy and its impact.
Challenge the underlying liability if no prior opportunity, request collection alternatives, and argue spousal defenses
Only discuss streamlined installment agreements and offers in compromise, with the IRS solely focusing on the taxpayer's ability to pay without considering other factors.
Request a new examination to re-evaluate the original tax assessment, even if the period for reassessment has expired and no new information has been presented.
explanation: CDP hearings allow: challenging the liability (if no prior opportunity), requesting innocent spouse relief, proposing collection alternatives (installment, OIC, CNC), and challenging lien/levy appropriateness. Settlement Officer handles. Why option B is CORRECT - At a CDP hearing, a taxpayer has the right to challenge the underlying tax liability if they did not receive a prior opportunity to dispute it (e.g., no statutory notice of deficiency). They can also propose collection alternatives like installment agreements, offers in compromise (OIC), or currently not collectible (CNC) status, and raise spousal defenses such as innocent spouse relief under IRC Section 6015. 💡 Exam Tip: Remember that the taxpayer's ability to challenge the underlying tax liability in a CDP hearing hinges on ...

question:
Following a series of violations of Circular 230, Emily Carter, an Enrolled Agent, has been disbarred from practice before the Treasury Department. What are the implications of this disbarment?

A) Is temporary
B) Only lasts 1 year
C) Is permanent unless the practitioner petitions for reinstatement after 5 years
D) Is automatic after any suspension
options: Is temporary, lasting only for the duration of the specific violation, after which practice is automatically reinstated.
Only lasts 1 year, after which the Enrolled Agent is automatically reinstated to practice before the IRS without further review.
Is permanent unless the practitioner petitions for reinstatement after 5 years
Is automatic after any suspension exceeding six months, regardless of the severity or nature of the initial infraction.
explanation: Disbarment removes practice privileges indefinitely. After at least 5 years, a disbarred individual may petition OPR for reinstatement by demonstrating fitness to practice. Why option C is CORRECT - Disbarment from practice before the IRS is permanent unless the individual petitions for reinstatement after a minimum of 5 years and demonstrates they are fit to practice. This is outlined in Circular 230 §10.81, which governs reinstatement after suspension or disbarment. 💡 Exam Tip: Remember the 5-year rule for reinstatement after disbarment; this is a frequently tested detail regarding Circular 230 compliance on the EA exam.

question: Maria discovered an error on her 2021 tax return and wants to amend it. She also needs to amend her 2022 return. Form 1040-X should be filed:
options: Electronically for current and up to 2 prior years
Through the IRS Online Account only, requiring ID.me verification for secure access.
Always on paper by mail, including all original schedules and supporting documentation.
Only with a tax professional who is an Enrolled Agent or Certified Public Accountant.
explanation: Form 1040-X can be e-filed for the current year and up to 3 prior tax years. Previously paper-only, e-filing was expanded. Some amended returns still require paper filing (e.g., certain elections). Why option A is CORRECT - Form 1040-X, Amended U.S. Individual Income Tax Return, can be filed electronically for the current tax year and the prior two tax years. This expansion of e-filing options aims to improve efficiency and taxpayer convenience, aligning with the IRS's modernization efforts. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific timeframes and methods allowed for filing various tax forms, especially those related to amended returns, as these are frequently tested.

question: Due to financial hardship, ABC Corp. negotiated an installment agreement with the IRS. As part of the agreement, the IRS granted a lien withdrawal. A lien withdrawal:
options: Is the same as a lien release, permanently removing the IRS's claim to the taxpayer's property and assets.
Eliminates interest and penalties accruing from the date the installment agreement is approved, providing immediate financial relief.
Releases the underlying tax debt, effectively forgiving the outstanding balance and terminating the IRS's collection efforts.
Removes the public filing but the lien itself remains in place
explanation: Withdrawal removes the NFTL from public record but the underlying lien continues. Release extinguishes the lien entirely. Taxpayers may request withdrawal if lien was filed prematurely or is impeding payment. Why option B is CORRECT - A lien withdrawal removes the Notice of Federal Tax Lien (NFTL) from public record, such as county recording offices. However, the underlying statutory tax lien itself remains in place, meaning the IRS still has a legal claim against the taxpayer's property. This is often done when the IRS believes it will facilitate collection, such as allowing the taxpayer to refinance a mortgage. 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific terminology used in lien-related questions. Differentiate between 'release,' 'withdrawal,' 'subordination,' and...

question: If a taxpayer has a net capital loss, the maximum amount that can be deducted against ordinary income is:
options: Unlimited
explanation: Net capital losses can offset ordinary income up to $3,000 per year ($1,500 if married filing separately). Any excess losses are carried forward to future years indefinitely. Why option D is CORRECT - Individuals can deduct up to $3,000 of net capital losses against ordinary income each year. If the net capital loss exceeds $3,000, the excess can be carried forward to future tax years indefinitely until it is fully used. This provision is found under IRC Section 1211(b). 💡 Exam Tip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the taxpayer's filing status when calculating the capital loss deduction, as the limit is halved for those married filing separately. Also, remember that capital losses first offset capital gains before being applied against ordinary income.
